coredata.dc:title: A novel 3D breast-cancer-on-chip platform for therapeutic evaluation of drug delivery systems
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yongli
Gao, Dan
Wang, Yanwei
Lin, Shuo
Jiang, Yuyang
coredata.dc:description: The ability to rapidly screen drugs and drug delivery systems with a more accurate tumor model to better predict their in vivo performance is of great importance in drug development, because there have been some limitations in currently used tumor models. To address this problem, we developed an in vitro breast tumor model on a chip, composed of a microvessel wall, the extracellular matrix (ECM) and uniformly sized multicellular tumor spheroids (MCTS), for the evaluation of nanoparticle-based drug delivery systems. A carbon dots (CDs)-based drug delivery system was synthesized as a model to evaluate the real-time monitoring ability of the system transport through the endothelium and the penetrability into MCTS with a high spatio-temporal resolution on the established platform. Moreover, a modified 96-well plate was used to hold the microfluidic devices for in situ cytotoxicity assays of the MCTS by a microplate reader. Our findings revealed that the synthesized drug delivery system could be transported across an endothelial monolayer within 3 h and was nontoxic to the cells throughout the experiment. In addition, we demonstrated the capabilities of this model by assessing the delivery and efficacy of the drug delivery system in BT549 and T47D spheroids, two cell lines representative of triple negative breast cancer (TNBC) and non-TNBC, respectively. This microfluidic platform enables evaluation of dynamic transport behavior and in situ cytotoxicity evaluation in one system. The established platform provides a more accurate and low-cost in vitro model for rapid drug screening in pre-clinical studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microfluidic chip
Multicellular tumor spheroids
Drug delivery
Drug screening

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of CDs, CDs-PEG, and CDs-PEG-FA
In vitro release kinetics of DOX
2D cell culture and cellular uptake of DOX evaluated by LC-MS/MS
3D tumor microenvironment model formation on the microfluidic device
Observation of tumor spheroids by confocal microscopy
In vitro behavior of the synthesized CDs-PEG-FA/DOX
Penetration of CDs-PEG-FA/DOX in the 3D breast tumor microenvironment model
Cytotoxicity of MCTS in the 3D breast tumor microenvironment model

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of the preparation of CDs-PEG-FA/DOX and the mechanism of killing tumor cells; (B) FOLRα expression levels in HUVECs, T47D, and BT549 cells evaluated by western blot. (C) Quantitative analysis of cellular uptake efficiency of DOX in HUVECs, T47D and BT549 cells by LC-MS/MS (n = 3; **p < 0.001, ***p < 0.0001). (D) Cytotoxicity assay results of T47D and BT549 cells after free DOX and CDs-PEG-FA/DOX treatment for 48 h.

(A) Schematic illustration of targeted delivery of nanomedicine to the tumor microenvironment. (B) Schematic illustration of the microfluidic chip which contains three microchannels, representing a capillary vessel, the ECM, and MCTS, respectively. The insert shows the dimensions of a U-shaped microchamber. (C) Perspective view of one of the U-shaped microchambers showing captured cells and nanoparticle-based drug delivery system. (D) The gas-liquid interfaces formed between channels a and b as well as b and c keep the BME inside the central channel. (E) Sectional view of the microfluidic device. (F) An image of the microfluidic device (top) and a micrograph showing the structure of the microchannels (bottom). (G) The workflow for CDs-based drug evaluation on the microfluidic platform.

Construction of the microvascular layer on the microfluidic device. (A) HUVECs were seeded on the BME surface to form an endothelial cell monolayer. (B) Fluorescent images of the endothelial monolayer immunostained with anti-VE-cadherin (green) and DAPI (blue), showing cell-cell interactions on the BME surfaces. (C) The enlarged image of (B). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Characterization of MCTS formation and long-term cultivation on the chip. The bright field images of BT549 spheroid formation at 12 h (A), 24 h (B), and 14 days (C). The bright field images of T47D spheroid formation at 12 h (E) and 24 h (F). The fluorescent images of a BT549 spheroid (D) and a T47D spheroids (G) on day 14 stained by a Live/Dead assay kit. (H) The average diameters of BT549 and T47D spheroids after 24 h and 14 days of culture (n = 5). (I) Bright field image of BT549 MCTS with uniform diameters grown on the microfluidic device.

(A) Fluorescent images of CDs-PEG-FA/DOX distribution on the HUVECs monolayer and the BME microchannel. CDs-PEG-FA/DOX with 5 μM of DOX were injected into the channel a, and the fluorescent images were taken at 0.5 h, 1 h and 3 h. (B) Bright field image of HUVECs monolayer after treatment with CDs-PEG-FA/DOX for 24 h. (C) Fluorescent image of the treated HUVECs stained by a Live/Dead assay kit.
Confocal images of the BT549 spheroids (A) and T47D spheroids (B) that have been exposed to CDs-PEG-FA/DOX for 6 h, 12 h, 24 h and 48 h.

Cytotoxicity assays of MCTS on a breast-cancer-on-chip platform. (A) Images of the modified well plate to hold the microfluidic devices for in situ drug cytotoxicity assays. The inhibition rates of BT549 spheroids (B) and T47D spheroids (C) after exposure to DOX, CDs-PEG/DOX, and CDs-PEG-FA/DOX (n = 3; p < 0.05; *, #, & represent 2-tailed Student's t-test analysis between CDs-PEG-FA/DOX and DOX treatment groups, CDs-PEG-FA/DOX and CDs-PEG/DOX treatment groups, and CDs-PEG/DOX and DOX treatment groups, respectively).

head.ce:title: A novel 3D breast-cancer-on-chip platform for therapeutic evaluation of drug delivery systems

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Oncogenomics, Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology, Graduate School at Shenzhen, Tsinghua University, Shenzhen, 518055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Oncogenomics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Chemical Genomics, School of Chemical Biology & Biotechnology, Graduate School at Shenzhen, Peking University, Shenzhen, 518055, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Metabolomics at Shenzhen, Graduate School at Shenzhen, Tsinghua University, Shenzhen, 518055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Metabolomics at Shenzhen

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of Metabolomics at Shenzhen, Graduate School at Shenzhen, Tsinghua University, Shenzhen, 518055, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Metabolomics at Shenzhen

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ability to rapidly screen drugs and drug delivery systems with a more accurate tumor model to better predict their in vivo performance is of great importance in drug development, because there have been some limitations in currently used tumor models. To address this problem, we developed an in vitro breast tumor model on a chip, composed of a microvessel wall, the extracellular matrix (ECM) and uniformly sized multicellular tumor spheroids (MCTS), for the evaluation of nanoparticle-based drug delivery systems. A carbon dots (CDs)-based drug delivery system was synthesized as a model to evaluate the real-time monitoring ability of the system transport through the endothelium and the penetrability into MCTS with a high spatio-temporal resolution on the established platform. Moreover, a modified 96-well plate was used to hold the microfluidic devices for in situ cytotoxicity assays of the MCTS by a microplate reader. Our findings revealed that the synthesized drug delivery system could be transported across an endothelial monolayer within 3 h and was nontoxic to the cells throughout the experiment. In addition, we demonstrated the capabilities of this model by assessing the delivery and efficacy of the drug delivery system in BT549 and T47D spheroids, two cell lines representative of triple negative breast cancer (TNBC) and non-TNBC, respectively. This microfluidic platform enables evaluation of dynamic transport behavior and in situ cytotoxicity evaluation in one system. The established platform provides a more accurate and low-cost in vitro model for rapid drug screening in pre-clinical studies.

A novel 3D breast-cancer-on-chip platform was developed to monitor in real-time the dynamic transport of nanoparticles and to perform cytotoxicity assays on one platform, which enables accurate prediction of drug release behavior for better preclinical drug evaluation.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, nanotechnology has been employed to deliver novel cancer therapeutics to improve the efficacy of cancer treatment by minimizing non-specific accumulation in normal tissue and systemic toxicity. Furthermore, drug delivery nanoparticles ranging from liposomes, nanotubes, nanorods, nanodots, micelles and polymers have been developed to enhance tumor targeting performance [1–3]. Although many of these nanoparticle-based drug delivery systems have been reported to improve therapeutic efficacy in vitro, examples of successful targeted delivery to solid tumors are limited [4,5], which is thought to be caused by the heterogeneity of tumor cells and pathophysiological barriers in the solid tumor microenvironment (TME). The solid TME is highly complex, and it poses several barriers for nanoparticle delivery: 1) In solid tumors, the vessels are often dense and show abnormal permeability; 2) The extracellular matrix (ECM) is highly developed by surrounding tumor cells to provide a transport barrier to the tumors; 3) Solid tumor cells grown as 3D multicellular aggregates display structural heterogeneity so that the nutrient and oxygen levels gradually decrease from the exterior to the center. All of these biological barriers might result in significant resistance to the delivery of the nanoparticle-based system through the interstitium and cause the drug to be released too far from the tumor cells [6,7]. Furthermore, the tumor heterogeneity with different subtypes might result in variable drug responses. For instance, triple negative breast cancer (TNBC) is a subtype of breast cancer that accounts for 15–20% of all breast cancer cases with a high rate of mortality because of its poor response to chemotherapy [8]. Therefore, drug response by cancer subtype should be a consideration for the improvement of personalized therapy development.

In fact, currently available tumor models do not properly represent the complex transport processes in TME. Animal models, which served as the gold standard for evaluating nanomedicine, face high economic and ethical burdens, and often fail to predict human responses during clinical trials [9]. Additionally, the traditional in vitro culture models are oversimplified and poorly simulate complex tissue architectures, thus leading to expensive failures at the late stages of drug development. Therefore, efforts were shifted towards developing multiple 3D culture systems, which could better recapitulate in vivo tissue functions for drug delivery system evaluation. Multicellular tumor spheroids (MCTS) are currently the most popularly used in vivo tumor model [10–13]. However, the established static MCTS models do not account for transport across the vascular endothelium structure [14–16]. Moreover, they focus exclusively on the response of tumor cells to drugs and do not allow real-time monitoring of nanoparticle transportation [17]. Recently, microfluidic technology has shown great potential to overcome the disadvantages mentioned above [18]. Unlike the conventional devices, the microfluidic platforms have enabled easy manipulation of liquid with microliter volumes and dynamic control of tumor-stroma communication [19–22], as well as tumor-ECM interaction [23,24]. However, they were limited in further drug screening studies, because the different sized MCTS present on the platform might lead to variable drug responses [25]. Recently, some microfluidic platforms have been developed to reconstruct 3D tumor spheroids using uniform structures to more accurately predict the drug efficiency [16,25–28]. However, they ignored the pathophysiological barriers of ECM and blood microvessels in the TME, which are also significant factors for the evaluation of drug delivery systems. Therefore, there is an urgent need to build a tumor model containing uniformly sized tumor spheroids, the ECM and endothelial cells on one platform for better drug evaluation. Regarding the existing on-chip drug efficacy evaluation methods, the majority were based on fluorescence detection after Live/Dead cell staining [29–32], and the cell survival rate was determined by counting the number of viable cells present in MCTS. Compared to the cytotoxicity assay by fluorescence detection on the chip, the detection by microplate reader will be more accurate.

Here, a breast cancer model, composed of an endothelial monolayer, the ECM, and uniformly sized MCTS, was established on a microfluidic device to investigate the key features of complex transport processes and cytotoxicity of a carbon dots (CDs)-based drug delivery system. In particular, CDs functionalized with polyethylene glycol (PEG) and folic acid (FA) were synthesized for doxorubicin (DOX) loading, and they exhibited great advantages for TNBC targeting in 2D culture. DOX was selected as the model drug because it is widely used in clinical treatment of various solid tumors like lung and breast cancer, as well as liquid tumors like leukemia and lymphomas. Compared with the reported platforms, this microfluidic model enabled the integration of uniform MCTS, the ECM, as well as an endothelial monolayer in one system, which better recapitulated the human breast cancer microenvironment. In addition, the established platform allowed for real-time monitoring of the drug delivery system transport across the microvascular wall and the ECM, as well as permeation into MCTS with a high spatio-temporal resolution. Moreover, the in situ cytotoxicity assay of the drug-treated MCTS could be subsequently evaluated in a more rapid and accurate mode by the microplate reader. In summary, we developed a highly biomimetic breast-cancer-on-chip model to monitor in real-time dynamic transport of nanoparticles and to perform cytotoxicity assays on one platform, which enables accurate prediction of drug release behavior for better preclinical drug evaluation.

The synthesis method of the nanocarrier CDs-PEG-FA is described in the Supporting Information. For the synthesis of CDs-PEG-FA/DOX, 1 mg mL−1 of CDs-PEG-FA was mixed with 2 mg mL−1 of DOX in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and stirred at room temperature in the dark overnight. The conjugates were separated from residual DOX by dialysis (MWCO 1000) against deionized (DI) water at room temperature for 48 h. The concentration of the released drug was determined by dissolving the drug-loaded conjugates and measuring the absorption at 480 nm. The drug loading content (DLC) was calculated based on the following equation:
(1)
D L C
( % )
=
t h e o r e t i c a l
a m o u n t
o f
d r u g
l o a d e d − f r e e
d r u g
weight
o f
carriers
× 100 %

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, CDs-PEG, and CDs-PEG-FA
ce:section.ce:para: CDs, CDs-PEG, and CDs-PEG-FA were characterized by the following techniques. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of products were recorded using a Beckman Coulter DU-800 spectrophotometer (Miami, FL, USA) at room temperature with a 1.0 cm path length. The fluorescence measurements were performed with a Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) using a 1.0 cm quartz cell equipped with a thermostatic bath. The emission slit and the exciting slit were both 2 nm. To confirm the functional groups after passivation, high-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-alpha, Thermo Fisher, Waltham, MA, USA) and FTIR (Equinox 55 Spectrometer, Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA) analyses were performed. The morphology and dimensional changes of CDs and CDs-PEG were studied via high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F30; FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro release kinetics of DOX
ce:section.ce:para: The release of DOX from CDs-PEG-FA conjugates was studied in different pH solutions at 37 °C. In detail, 200 μL of 1 mg mL−1 CDs-PEG-FA/DOX were transferred into a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1000). The dialysis membrane was stirred at 37 °C in 0.1 M PBS with pH values of 6.4 and 7.4, respectively. At desired time intervals, 1 mL of released media was taken out for UV measurement at 480 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 2D cell culture and cellular uptake of DOX evaluated by LC-MS/MS
ce:section.ce:para: The primary human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) were isolated from an umbilical cord and grown in endothelial cell growth medium (EGM-2 BulletKit, Lonza, Walkersville, MD, USA) supplemented with 2% fetal bovine serum (FBS) in a 37 °C incubator with 5% CO2. In addition, the human breast cancer cell lines T47D and BT549, obtained from the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China), were cultured in RPMI-1640 medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum, 100 μg mL−1 penicillin, and 100 μg mL−1 streptomycin at 37 °C in a humidified incubator with 5% CO2.

The cellular uptakes of DOX by HUVECs, T47D and BT549 cells were investigated by liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS). To prepare samples for analysis, 5 × 106 cells were exposed to CDs-PEG-FA/DOX at a final DOX concentration of 5 μM. After 24 h incubation, the culture medium was removed, and the cells were washed gently with DI water. For extraction, plates were immediately transferred to an ice bath and 1 mL of ice-cold 80% aqueous methanol was added to each plate and cells were scraped with a cell lifter. The cell pellets were transferred to micro-centrifuge tubes, ultrasonicated for 10 min in an ice bath, and subsequently centrifuged at 4 °C for 10 min at 16,000 × g. Supernatants were collected and dried overnight using a Scientz-10 N freeze-dryer (Ningbo Scientz Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China). The dried residue was reconstituted with 200 μL of 30% Acetonitrile. Then, 10 μL of the supernatant solution were injected into the LC-MS/MS for the quantitation of DOX. The equipment conditions are described in the Supporting Information.

The microfluidic device was fabricated from 2 layers of PDMS (Sylgard 184, Dow Corning, Midland, MI) using standard soft lithography and replica molding techniques described previously [25]. The detailed processes of the device fabrication are shown in the Supporting Information. The upper layer contained three independent microchannels, a capillary vessel channel (channel a), a connected ECM channel (channel b) and an MCTS culture channel (channel c). In detail, the channel b was 1 mm wide and 40 μm high, and the others were both 1 mm wide and 100 μm high. Channel c contained 11 U-shaped microchambers that could retain the tumor cells for the formation of MCTS. To allow perfusion flow and avoid cells escaping, a channel with 10 μm depth in the bottom layer was designed and aligned with the U-shaped microchambers. 10 mg mL−1 Pluronic F127 (Sigma, St. Louis, MO, USA) in DI water was introduced into the channel with U-shaped microchambers and incubated for 2 h in order to prevent cell attachment. Before cell seeding, the microfluidic system was sterilized by exposure to UV light for 30 min and then the microchannels were flushed with 70% ethyl alcohol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3D tumor microenvironment model formation on the microfluidic device
ce:section.ce:para: For the 3D culture experiment, basement membrane extract (BME) was thawed at 4 °C in a refrigerator overnight and loaded into the connecting channel of the microfluidic device to serve as a substitute for the ECM. Driven by surface tension, the BME stopped at the interface of the connection channel with channels a and c and gelled within 1 h. Next, HUVECs were loaded into channel a. The device was then tilted to an upright position and incubated for approximately 4 h to allow the cells to settle along the BME surface. Next, the device was returned to its original position for tumor spheroid formation. In detail, channel c was firstly modified with 1% polyvinyl alcohol to prevent cell adhesion onto the microchannel surface. Then 10 μL of a ∼5 × 106 cells mL−1 cell suspension were injected into the microchannel using a pipette at a low flow rate for cell trapping. The cells were further cultured to form tumor spheroids. The growth and aggregation of the tumor cells were measured under an inverted light microscope (Olympus CKX41, Olympus Co. Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). After 14 days of cultivation, the cell viability was evaluated on the chip using a Live/Dead assay kit.

After washing with PBS, HUVECs on the chip were fixed in 4% (w/v) paraformaldehyde for 15 min and then permeabilized using a 0.15% Triton X-100 solution for 5 min. After blocking with 3% bovine serum albumin (BSA) for 20 min, samples were first incubated with the primary antibody for VE-cadherin (Cell Signalling, USA) overnight at 4 °C, and then incubated with FITC-conjugated secondary antibodies (Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology, Shanghai, China) at room temperature. Nuclei were stained by 4',6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) for 5 min. The samples were then washed with PBS to remove excess antibodies. They were viewed under a Leica DMI 4000 B fluorescent microscope (Wetzlar, Germany) and analyzed by Image-Pro Plus 6.0 software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation of tumor spheroids by confocal microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the penetration ability, CDs-PEG-FA/DOX with a final DOX concentration of 5 μM were injected into channel a. After treatment for 6 h, 12 h, and 24 h, the spheroids were rinsed with PBS several times and analyzed using an Olympus FV1000-IX81 confocal laser-scanning microscope (Olympus, Tokyo, Japan). Z-stack images were obtained by scanning the tumor spheroids step-by-step. The scanning began from the top of a spheroid. Each scanning layer was 15 μm in thickness, and the total scan was approximately 100 μm in depth for a spheroid.

Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8, Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology, Shanghai, China) was used for in situ cell viability assay of MCTS. After drug exposure for 48 h, 10 μL of the CCK-8 solution was added to channel c and incubated for 1 h at 37 °C. Then, two areas of a 96-well plate were modified to hold four microfluidic devices each. The absorbance was measured at 450 nm by a microplate reader (Tecan Infinite M1000 pro, Mannedorf, Switzerland).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro behavior of the synthesized CDs-PEG-FA/DOX
ce:section.ce:para: The used fluorescent CDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method, and their surfaces were passivated with amino-group terminated PEG (PEG-NH2), which could be engineered for further conjugation with FA (Fig. 1 A). Specifically, the formation of CDs-PEG-FA nanoparticles was confirmed by UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR spectrometer, XPS and TEM (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). The detailed characterization results are discussed in the Supporting Information. DOX is one of the most common chemotherapeutic agents used for breast cancer treatment, but its clinical application is limited due to severe side effects and drug resistance [33,34]. In this work, CDs-PEG-FA were employed as nanocarriers for DOX to improve the breast cancer targeting and drug delivery ability. The DOX was encapsulated into the nanocarrier CDs-PEG-FA with a drug loading content of 82.5 wt%. The drug-loading amount was found to be greater than previously reported results [35]. This could be due to the large surface area and porous nature of CDs. Additionally, DOX released from CDs-PEG-FA showed pH-dependent profiles, and release was significantly promoted at pH 6.4 (Fig. S2, Supporting Information), which might be attributed to the reversible stretching and collapsing of PEG molecules under different pH values [36]. As the microenvironment found in most solid tumors is acidic, the DOX release is expected to be enhanced by acidic pH, thereby improving the therapeutic effect on tumors. Additionally, specific ligand-mediated targeting may be a feasible strategy for tumor targeting. It has been reported that folate receptor α (FOLRα), upregulated in TNBC patients, plays a significant role in malignant cell proliferation, migration, and invasion [37,38]. In this context, 2D cultures of three cell lines with different expression levels of FOLRα (Fig. 1B), the normal cells HUVECs, non-TNBC cells T47D and TNBC cells BT549, were employed to evaluate the selective targeting ability of CDs-PEG-FA/DOX using LC-MS/MS. As shown in Fig. 1C, the uptakes of DOX differed significantly among the three types of cells after 24 h treatment. The concentration of DOX in BT549 cells reached about 280 nM per 106 cells, distinctly higher than those in HUVECs (11 nM per 106 cells) and T47Ds cells (90 nM per 106 cells). The anticancer efficiencies of CDs-PEG-FA/DOX and DOX toward TNBC cells and non-TNBC cells were further investigated by MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 1D, free DOX presented similar cytotoxicity effect on the two cell lines, with an IC50 value of approximately 5 μM. However, CDs-PEG-FA/DOX exhibited higher cytotoxicity for BT549 cells compared with T47D cells because of their higher FOLRα expression, with an IC50 value of approximately 1 μM. Taken together, the results suggested that the controlled release of DOX from CDs-PEG-FA/DOX drug delivery system had superior apoptosis ability against TNBC cells over non-TNBC cells.

In order to recapitulate the complex transport processes in tumors, a 3D microfluidic model was designed to mimic the tumor microenvironment, where the ECM and MCTS were surrounded by capillary vessel cells (Fig. 2 A). As shown in Fig. 2B and E, the microfluidic device contained three parallel channels: channel a for capillary vessel formation, channel b for the ECM formation, and channel c for MCTS formation in the U-shaped microchambers. The dimensions of the microchamber were optimized, with 300 μm (length) × 200 μm (width) × 100 μm (height) obtaining better cell trapping performance (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). Fig. 2F shows an image of the microfluidic device (top) and a micrograph shows the structure of the microchannels (bottom). Moreover, in order to improve the cell trapping efficiency, a flat channel with 10 μm depth in the bottom layer was designed to align with the U-shaped microchambers, allowing fluid flow under the microchambers and reducing backflow (Fig. 2C). Compared to some other methods [25,39], this cell-trapping technique for MCTS formation could prevent the spheroids from flowing out during the culture medium exchange procedure. In addition, the height of channel b was designed to be lower than those of channels a and c in order to generate gas-liquid interfaces (Fig. 2D). A theoretical calculation [40] showed that the surface tension-induced pressure at the end positions of the gas-liquid interface was only 1.29 Pa (Fig. S4, Supporting Information). The pressure was much smaller than atmospheric pressure so that the BME could not leak into the adjacent channels. In order to further confirm that the liquid could diffuse easily from channel a to c, a fluorescein sodium diffusion assay was performed. As expected, a fluorescence balance among the three channels was achieved within 180 min, demonstrating the feasibility of this microfluidic device for the CDs-based drug transport (Fig. S5, Supporting Information). Using this microfluidic platform, in situ CDs-based drug evaluation, including cytotoxicity assays and real-time drug delivery monitoring, could be realized (Fig. 2G).

BME was loaded into the middle microchannel and served as the perivascular ECM. After polymerization, the HUVECs were seeded into channel a to mimic a microvascular layer. In order to help HUVECs attach to the BME surface and prevent cell adhesion to PDMS, a 10 mg mL−1 Pluronic F127 solution was used to modify the surface of the microchannels for 2 h at room temperature. Without treatment, HUVECs adhered to both PDMS and BME (Fig. S6); however with treatment, HUVECs only adhered to the surface of the BME (Fig. 3 A). The cell-cell interaction in the HUVECs monolayer was confirmed by VE-cadherin immunostaining (Fig. 3B and C), indicating a biomimetic microvessel wall was formed. Compared to the construction of microvessel walls with nanoporous membrane [29] or endothelial cells [32], the use of HUVECs cells has a higher biomimetic effect. In order to form uniformly sized MCTS in the U-shaped microchambers, BT549 cells at a high concentration were injected. As expected, the loaded cells gradually aggregated into tumor spheroids in the center due to the non-adherent coating. After 12 h of cultivation, preliminary cell aggregation was clearly observed (Fig. 4 A). The aggregation then became tighter and tighter, and compact MCTS were generated after 24 h with an average diameter of 120 μm (Fig. 4B and H). The MCTS continuously grew, and the average diameter was approximately 180 μm after 14 days of cultivation (Fig. 4C and H). It is worth noting that the cell viability of the spheroids was over 90% by Live/Dead staining assay, which demonstrated that the microfluidic device was suitable for long-term MCTS cultivation (Fig. 4D). Using the same procedure, T47D cells were gathered together to form a spheroid over 12 h (Fig. 4E) and form a tight spheroid after 24 h cultivation with an average diameter of approximately 125 μm (Fig. 4F and H). Long-term cultivation of T47D cells was also achieved on the microfluidic platform (Fig. 4G). The results indicated that the presented platform could be widely applied for other tumor cell lines. Moreover, since the loaded cells were evenly distributed in the microchambers, uniformly sized MCTS could be formed (Fig. 4I and Fig. S7 in the Supporting Information). Compared to previously developed platforms [26,41], our established microfluidic model could integrate an endothelial layer, the ECM, as well as uniformly sized MCTS into one system, which exhibited great potential for drug delivery evaluation and cytotoxicity screening.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Penetration of CDs-PEG-FA/DOX in the 3D breast tumor microenvironment model
ce:section.ce:para: The endothelial monolayer and the ECM are the major penetration barriers for intravenously administered anticancer drugs in reaching tumor cells [42]. To simulate this, CDs-PEG-FA/DOX were injected into channel a to assess their transport across the HUVECs monolayer and the BME hydrogel. Interestingly, a slight fluorescence was observed in the hydrogel at 0.5 h, and fluorescent signal balance between the channels a and b was achieved after 3 h (Fig. 5 A). The relative quantitative results of the fluorescent signal changes were shown in Fig. S8, Supporting Information. The result confirmed the high penetrability of this CDs-based drug delivery system to transport across the endothelial monolayer and the ECM, which was consistent with previously reported results [43]. After treatment for 24 h, HUVECs on the BME surface (Fig. 5B) were stained by a Live/Dead assay kit and cells exhibited a high survival rate (Fig. 5C). The result indicated that CDs-PEG-FA/DOX had no effect on endothelial cells. Furthermore, they were applied to investigate the penetration through the endothelial monolayer and the ECM to MCTS. As shown in Fig. 6 A, CDs-PEG-FA/DOX was present in the periphery of BT549 spheroids after 12 h, and gradually penetrated into the center of MCTS after 48 h. However, the rate and extent of the drug delivery system diffusion into T47D spheroids exhibited poor performance, with CDs-PEG-FA/DOX only accumulating in the periphery of MCTS after treatment for 48 h (Fig. 6B). Thus, our data strongly suggested that the uptake of CDs-PEG-FA/DOX by BT549 was facilitated by FLORα, which has the highest affinity for folic acid. Consistent with current research, the CDs-PEG-FA/DOX showed greater penetrability into TNBC cells, which might make them a good choice to improve TNBC treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of MCTS in the 3D breast tumor microenvironment model
ce:section.ce:para: For further in situ drug screening assays, the arrays of U-shaped microchambers were also designed to align with the measurement wells for spectrofluorimetric microplate readers. Specifically, a 96-well plate was modified to hold eight microfluidic devices in two groups of four (Fig. 7 A). The U-shaped microchambers of each chip aligned to one well. A spectral reading of one well corresponded to the 11 MCTS on one chip. Dose-dependent cytotoxicity was observed for free DOX, CDs-PEG/DOX, and CDs-PEG-FA/DOX in both BT549 and T47D spheroids. Additionally, CDs-PEG-FA/DOX exhibited better inhibition of BT549 and T47D spheroids proliferation than free DOX or CDs-PEG/DOX, suggesting these CDs-based nanoparticles could serve as an efficient drug delivery nanocarrier (Fig. 7B and C). Similar to the research [44] and the MTT assay of 2D cells presented in Fig. 1D, the nano-drug delivery system showed greater sensitivity to TNBC BT549 spheroids than non-TNBC T47D spheroids. This phenomenon might be due to the higher level of FOLRα expression in BT549 cells. The above results indicated that our established platform has great potential to integrate with microplate readers for on-line drug screening with a more accurate and rapid performance.

In this study, we developed a 3D breast tumor microenvironment model on a microfluidic device for rapid evaluation of drug delivery systems. Compared with previously reported platforms, this microfluidic model had some important advantages: (1) The microfluidic platform enabled formation of uniformly sized MCTS; (2) MCTS, the ECM, as well as the endothelial monolayer could be integrated into one system, which better recapitulated the human breast cancer microenvironment for more accurate drug screening; (3) It allowed for real-time monitoring of the drug delivery system transport across the microvascular wall, the ECM, as well as permeation into MCTS with high spatio-temporal resolution; (4) In situ cytotoxicity assays could be performed with high-throughput. The established platform was the first attempt to combine real-time transportation monitoring and in situ cytotoxicity assays in one system for drug delivery system evaluation. In 2D cell culture, CDs-PEG-FA/DOX were shown to be an excellent nanoparticle-based drug delivery system for TNBC cells. Fluorescence imaging revealed that CDs-PEG-FA/DOX penetrated into BT549 spheroids better than T47D spheroids, resulting in higher cytotoxicity towards BT549 MCTS. Further work will include integration of other aspects of TME including hypoxia, interstitial flow and various stromal cells into the 3D breast tumor microenvironment model to better replicate the in vivo microenvironment for more accurate evaluation of nanomedicines.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Sarah E. Noll in the Department of Chemistry at Stanford University for her assistance in editing the manuscript. Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21675096 and 21475073), and Youth Scientific Research Funds from Graduate School at Shenzhen, Tsinghua University (No. QN20160002) are highly appreciated.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.06.038.
